## From regression to classification

Linear regression predicts a continuous value. Classification predicts a discrete
label. The simplest bridge between the two is a linear model followed by a threshold
function: compute a continuous score, then assign the class based on whether the
score exceeds a cutoff.

## The perceptron

The perceptron (Rosenblatt, 1958) is the simplest neural unit. It computes a
weighted sum of the inputs, applies a step function, and outputs a binary label:

$$
\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b \geq 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The perceptron learning rule updates the weights only when a prediction is wrong.
It is guaranteed to converge if the data is linearly separable, but it never
converges if the classes overlap.

## Adaline: adaptive linear neurons

Adaline (Widrow & Hoff, 1960) improves on the perceptron by using a continuous
activation during training. The net input is

$$
z^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b
$$

and the training loss is the mean squared error between $z^{(i)}$ and the true
label $y^{(i)}$. The threshold function is applied only at prediction time, not
during weight updates.

::: {.teacher-only}
Stress the key difference: the perceptron updates on the thresholded output
($y - \hat{y}$), while Adaline updates on the continuous residual ($y - z$). This
makes Adaline's loss surface smooth and gradient-descent-friendly.
:::

## Why Adaline matters

Adaline uses exactly the same gradient descent machinery as linear regression. The
only addition is the threshold at prediction time. This makes it the conceptual
bridge between regression and neural networks:

1. Linear regression: continuous output, MSE loss, gradient descent.
2. Adaline: continuous training signal, threshold at prediction, MSE loss, gradient
   descent.
3. Logistic regression: sigmoid output, cross-entropy loss, gradient descent.
4. Multi-layer perceptron: stacked layers of non-linear activations.

Each step adds one idea while keeping the optimization framework intact.

## Decision boundaries

Both the perceptron and Adaline produce linear decision boundaries. In two
dimensions, the boundary is a line; in higher dimensions, it is a hyperplane. When
classes are not linearly separable, these models cannot achieve zero training error.

Feature standardization matters here just as it does for regression: when features
have different scales, the optimization landscape is distorted and gradient descent
converges slowly or not at all.

## Limitations

Linear classifiers fail when the true decision boundary is curved. Logistic
regression and multi-layer perceptrons address this by introducing non-linear
activation functions and stacked layers, which are covered in later lectures.
